title: Document Augmentation via Question Generation
one_liner: Enrich the vector index at build time by generating synthetic questions for each document/fragment with an LLM, so user queries match question-shaped vectors instead of only declarative prose.
problem_solved: Embedding similarity between a short interrogative user query and long declarative document text is often weak (question-vs-statement style mismatch), so the right chunk ranks poorly. This technique closes that gap by indexing LLM-generated questions alongside the original fragments: a user question now matches a semantically near-identical synthetic question, which carries metadata pointing back to its full parent document for answer generation.
failure_modes: Users phrase queries as questions but the corpus is declarative prose, so vector search misses relevant sections (query/corpus vocabulary and style mismatch)
The right chunk exists but ranks below irrelevant chunks because its wording shares few terms or embedding-space features with the query
Small chunks retrieve precisely but lack surrounding context needed for the LLM to answer (mitigated here by storing the full parent document in each vector's metadata)
One document section must be findable from many differently-phrased user questions, but a single embedding per chunk can only be close to a few of them
when_to_use: Your users ask natural-language questions against a corpus of explanatory or report-style prose (FAQs, manuals, policy docs, textbooks)
Retrieval evaluation shows the answer-bearing chunk exists but is not in the top-k for question-form queries
The corpus is small-to-medium and mostly static, so a one-time LLM augmentation pass at index build is affordable
You can anticipate the space of user questions well enough that generated questions will cover real query phrasings
You want a retrieve-small/read-big pattern: match on a tiny fragment or question, answer from the full parent document
tradeoffs: Index build cost: one LLM call per document (or per fragment at FRAGMENT_LEVEL) generating up to 40 questions each — cost and build time scale linearly with corpus size
Index size balloons: ~40 extra vectors per document plus all original fragments, increasing embedding cost, storage, and potential retrieval noise from redundant/generic questions
Question quality is unvalidated: generic or hallucination-adjacent questions can match unrelated queries and route to the wrong parent document
Stale on update: any document edit requires regenerating its questions and re-embedding; poor fit for frequently changing corpora
Notebook implementation is fragile: k=1 retrieval leaves no room for a bad top hit, and chunking uses a crude regex word tokenizer rather than real token counts
Not needed when queries already share the corpus's phrasing (e.g., keyword-heavy technical lookup) or when a query-side fix like HyDE/query rewriting is cheaper than augmenting a huge index
how_it_works: A PDF is read to a string (read_pdf_to_string helper) and split twice with a regex word-tokenizer: first into large 'text documents' (DOCUMENT_MAX_TOKENS=4000, overlap 100) for answer context, then each into small 'fragments' (FRAGMENT_MAX_TOKENS=128, overlap 16) for retrieval. For each document (or each fragment, controlled by a QuestionGeneration enum), ChatOpenAI (gpt-4o-mini, temperature 0) with structured output (a pydantic QuestionList) generates at least QUESTIONS_PER_DOCUMENT=40 candidate questions, which are cleaned (numbered prefixes stripped, must end in '?') and deduplicated. Both original fragments (metadata type ORIGINAL) and generated questions (type AUGMENTED) become LangChain Documents whose metadata['text'] holds the full parent document. All are embedded with OpenAIEmbeddings into a FAISS store; a retriever with k=1 finds the nearest vector — usually an augmented question — and the answer LLM is prompted with the parent document from metadata rather than the matched snippet. The technique operates primarily at indexing time but implies a post-retrieval step (parent-document lookup) and a generation step (answer from parent context).
key_dependencies: OpenAI API (gpt-4o-mini for question generation and answering; text embeddings)
key_code_signals: class QuestionGeneration(Enum): DOCUMENT_LEVEL / FRAGMENT_LEVEL — switch for augmentation granularity
QUESTIONS_PER_DOCUMENT = 40 with DOCUMENT_MAX_TOKENS=4000 / FRAGMENT_MAX_TOKENS=128 two-level split
Document(page_content=question, metadata={'type': 'AUGMENTED', 'text': text_document}) — questions indexed as first-class vectors carrying parent text
FAISS.from_documents(documents, embedding_model) over mixed ORIGINAL + AUGMENTED docs
context = doc.metadata['text'] then generate_answer(context, query) — answer from parent document, not the matched vector's text
clean_and_filter_questions: regex-strip numbering, keep only strings ending with '?'
related_techniques: HyDE (hyde_hypothetical_document_embedding): alternative — mirror-image approach that generates a hypothetical document for the query at search time instead of questions for documents at index time
HyPE (HyPE_Hypothetical_Prompt_Embeddings): refines — same hypothetical-question idea, formalized as replacing chunk embeddings with question embeddings at index time
query_transformations: alternative — fixes query/corpus mismatch on the query side with rewriting/step-back/decomposition, no index rebuild needed
context_enrichment_window_around_chunk: composes — both use retrieve-small/read-big; window enrichment expands context after retrieval instead of storing parent text in metadata
hierarchical_indices: related — shares the two-level document/fragment structure for retrieval vs context
proposition_chunking: alternative — augments the index with LLM-derived atomic propositions instead of synthetic questions
contextual_chunk_headers: composes — another index-time augmentation that prepends context to chunks; can be combined with question generation
reranking: composes — mitigates this notebook's fragile k=1 retrieval by retrieving more candidates and reranking
summary: Index-time document augmentation that attacks the question-vs-prose embedding mismatch in RAG: user queries are questions, but corpus chunks are declarative text, so nearest-neighbor search often misses the answer-bearing section. The notebook splits a PDF into large context documents (4000 tokens) and small retrieval fragments (128 tokens), then uses gpt-4o-mini with pydantic structured output to generate ~40 answerable questions per document (or per fragment). Generated questions are cleaned, deduplicated, and indexed in FAISS as first-class vectors alongside original fragments, each tagged AUGMENTED and carrying the full parent document in metadata. At query time the top hit is usually a synthetic question nearly identical to the user's; the pipeline then answers from the parent document stored in that hit's metadata (retrieve-small, read-big). Costs: one LLM call per document at build, ~40x more vectors, staleness on document updates, and unvalidated question quality. Best for static, question-driven corpora (FAQs, manuals, reports) where recall failures come from phrasing mismatch; query-side alternatives are HyDE or query rewriting, and HyPE is the closest sibling technique.
